caption_text:
Buzzing through Bay to Breakers 🐝

From casual campus runs to intercollegiate competitions, the Stanford Running Club (@stanford.running.club) builds community for runners of all abilities through weekly runs, workouts, and races.

Claire Swihart (@claire.swihart), ’29, joined the club at Bay to Breakers, one of San Francisco’s largest annual races, to uphold the tradition of running in costumes while connected together. This year’s costume? Buzzing bees.

caption_text:
Some moments are meant to be. 💫

When Sofia Islas (@sofiislas_ ), ‘28, was 11, she discovered BTS through their song “Blood Sweat & Tears.” Years later, she wrote her Stanford application essay about the band. This week, she biked from her dorm to see them perform at Stanford Stadium.

One of the surprise songs on night two? “Blood Sweat & Tears.”

🎥: @sofiislas_ | @laloespinozasan

caption_text:
A week centered on women’s health 🩺

Stanford Women’s Health Initiative (@whi_stanford) brought together students, faculty, clinicians, founders, and investors from across Stanford for its inaugural Women’s Health Week, held May 9–15.

caption_text:
In 2018, we invited Stanford alums to write short notes to our incoming students. Here’s what Jason Collins shared.

Collins, ’01, led @stanfordmbb to the 1998 Final Four as a freshman and became the second Stanford basketball player in school history to grace the cover of Sports Illustrated. He went on to a 13-year career in the @nba and was inducted into the Stanford Hall of Fame in 2017 alongside his brother Jarron. Collins became the first openly gay player in any of the four major American men’s sports leagues when he came out in 2013.

Collins died Tuesday following a battle with glioblastoma. He was 47.

“Jason was a trailblazer whose influence, determination, and courage far transcended the game of basketball,” said John Donahoe, the Jaquish & Kenninger Director and Chair of Athletics. “His spirit was a guiding light and he will be forever cherished by the Stanford community.”

📷: Stanford Athletics

Not your average team meeting 🃏

Watch Jake Hofman (@itsjakemagic), ’27, transfer student and campus magician, stun @stanfordfball.

caption_text:
Some moments are worth slowing down for ✍️

Matthew Yu (@matthewyuart), ’29, sketched the floating lantern ceremony at Flourish Fest as students gathered for an evening of art, music, and connection.

caption_text:
Celebrating 55 years of Stanford Powwow (@thestanfordpowwow).

The annual student-produced event returned to the Eucalyptus Grove this weekend as a celebration of Indigenous traditions and community.

caption_text:
Meet the 2026 cohort of Knight-Hennessy scholars!

These 87 new scholars make up the most global Knight-Hennessy Scholars cohort to date. They will pursue degrees in 45 graduate programs across all seven graduate schools at Stanford while engaging in experiences that prepare them to be visionary, courageous, and collaborative leaders capable of taking on the world’s most difficult challenges.

More about the 2026 cohort:
➡️ The 87 scholars are citizens of 31 countries, including the first scholars from Croatia, Portugal, United Arab Emirates, and Uzbekistan
➡️ Scholars have earned undergraduate degrees at 58 different institutions, including 23 institutions outside of the U.S. and 21 institutions represented for the first time
➡️ 21% of scholars are the first in their family to graduate from college

Congratulations, and a warm KHS welcome! 🔗 in bio to learn more about the 2026 cohort
Before the red carpets and the awards, Sterling K. Brown (@sterlingkbrown), ’98, and Ryan Michelle Bathe (@ryanmichelleb), ’98, were just two frosh auditioning for their first play on the Farm.
